# Go Unsafe Module

## Overview
Go unsafe package provides operations that bypass Go's type system and memory safety, primarily used for low-level system programming and performance optimization.

## Files

### basics.mdx
- **Unsafe Basics**: Introduction to unsafe package, unsafe.Pointer and uintptr types, Sizeof/Alignof/Offsetof functions, safety warnings, and use cases (high-performance scenarios, system programming, low-level optimization, standard library implementation)
- Pointer conversion rules: *T1 ↔ unsafe.Pointer ↔ *T2 (same size), unsafe.Pointer ↔ uintptr
- uintptr dangers: GC may move objects, don't store uintptr for long
- Real-world applications: zero-copy string/bytes conversion, slice to array pointer, atomic operations

### pointer-operations.mdx
- **Pointer Operations**: Advanced pointer operations with unsafe
- Type conversion: same-size type conversion, pointer type conversion
- Array/slice operations: array to slice conversion, slice to array conversion, zero-length slice tricks
- String operations: zero-copy string to bytes conversion (read-only), zero-copy bytes to string conversion
- Struct operations: direct struct field access, struct slice tricks
- Union simulation: using unsafe to simulate C unions

### memory-layout.mdx
- **Memory Layout**: Understanding Go type memory layout
- Basic types layout: bool (1 byte), integers (1/2/4/8 bytes), floats (4/8 bytes), string (16 bytes: ptr+len), slice (24 bytes: ptr+len+cap), interface (16 bytes: type+data)
- Struct layout: alignment rules, padding, field reordering optimization
- Array layout: fixed-size arrays, multi-dimensional arrays, memory contiguity
- Slice layout: slice header, relationship with underlying array, multiple slices sharing array
- Map layout: hmap structure, bucket structure, memory usage
- Channel layout: hchan structure, unbuffered vs buffered channels
- Interface layout: empty interface (eface), non-empty interface (iface)
- Memory layout optimization: reducing memory footprint, using smaller integer types

### best-practices.mdx
- **Best Practices**: When and how to use unsafe properly
- When to use: performance-critical paths, C library interaction (CGO), standard-library level features, system programming
- When NOT to use: application code, normal optimization (use pprof), cases where interfaces work, code generation alternatives
- Common pitfalls: storing uintptr too long, object moving after conversion, modifying read-only memory, different type sizes, unaligned access
- Safe patterns: restricted unsafe usage, boundary checking, documenting constraints
- Performance optimization: caching offsets, batch operations
- CGO integration: passing pointers to C, receiving pointers from C
- Testing unsafe code: unit tests, race detection, fuzzing
- Debugging: memory dump, alignment verification
- Alternatives: using interfaces, code generation, standard library

## Related Topics
- Go reflection (../reflection/): Runtime type inspection
- Go concurrency (../concurrency/): Atomic operations
- Go data types (../data-types/): Type system overview
